bytes.title: Redis Cache-Aside — Cut DB Load with GET/SETEX
bytes.body:
Every hot query hitting PostgreSQL directly is wasted work. Redis fixes this with the cache-aside pattern: your app checks Redis first, falls back to the DB on a miss, then writes the result back to Redis with a TTL. The key insight is that Redis never pulls data automatically — your code owns the read-through and write logic, which gives you precise control over what gets cached and for how long.

Use `GET` to check the cache. On a miss, query the DB, then call `SETEX key ttl value` to store it — atomically setting both value and expiry in one command. This prevents stale entries from living forever and avoids the thundering herd: if your TTL is short, expiries are staggered naturally across instances.

For user profile lookups (read-heavy, rarely mutated), a 60-second TTL with `SETEX user:1234 60 '{...}'` can eliminate 90%+ of DB reads. On writes, call `DEL user:1234` immediately — don't update the cache, invalidate it. This avoids stale reads without complex cache-update logic.

bytes.topic: Why Queues — decoupling, async processing, load leveling
bytes.title: Redis as a Queue — Decoupling Services with LPUSH/BRPOP
bytes.body: When your API endpoint triggers a slow operation — sending email, resizing images, hitting a third-party API — executing it inline blocks the response and couples your services tightly. Redis lists solve this cleanly. The producer calls `LPUSH jobs:email '{...}'` and returns immediately. The consumer runs in a separate process, blocking on `BRPOP jobs:email 0` — the `0` timeout means it waits indefinitely, consuming zero CPU until a job arrives. This is load leveling: a traffic spike pushes 10,000 items onto the list while your consumer drains at its own pace, preventing cascading failures. For priority queues, use multiple lists with `BRPOP jobs:critical jobs:normal 0` — Redis pops from the first non-empty list, giving `critical` natural priority. Need visibility? `LLEN jobs:email` gives queue depth instantly. For at-least-once delivery, use `RPOPLPUSH jobs:email jobs:email:processing` to atomically move the job to a processing list before working it, then `LREM jobs:email:processing 1 '{...}'` on success — surviving crashes without lost jobs.

bytes.topic: Message Delivery Guarantees — at-least-once vs at-most-once vs exactly-once
bytes.title: Redis Message Delivery Guarantees — at-least-once, at-most-once, and exactly-once
bytes.body:
Redis Streams give you fine-grained control over delivery semantics depending on how you use consumer groups and ACKs.

**At-most-once:** Read with XREAD (no consumer group). Messages are consumed and never redelivered — if your consumer crashes before processing, the message is lost. Fast, but lossy.

**At-least-once:** Use XREADGROUP + XACK. Messages land in the Pending Entries List (PEL) on delivery. If the consumer dies before calling XACK, XAUTOCLAIM or XCLAIM reassigns them after a timeout. You may process the same message twice — idempotency is your responsibility.

**Exactly-once:** Redis has no native exactly-once. You simulate it by combining XREADGROUP with a Redis transaction: use a Lua script or MULTI/EXEC to atomically process the message and write a deduplication key (SET msg:<id> 1 EX 86400 NX). If the key already exists, skip processing and still XACK. This prevents double-processing while keeping the PEL clean.

For most backends, at-least-once + idempotent handlers is the pragmatic sweet spot.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Lua script: exactly-once processing via dedup key + XACK
local dedup_key = 'processed:' .. ARGV[1]  -- ARGV[1] = stream message ID
local already = redis.call('SET', dedup_key, '1', 'EX', 86400, 'NX')
if already == false then
  -- already processed, just ACK and return
  redis.call('XACK', KEYS[1], KEYS[2], ARGV[1])
  return 0
end
-- safe to process now
-- ... your domain logic here ...
redis.call('XACK', KEYS[1], KEYS[2], ARGV[1])
return 1

bytes.topic: Idempotency — designing consumers to safely handle duplicate messages
bytes.title: Redis Idempotency — Designing Consumers to Safely Handle Duplicate Messages
bytes.body:
Redis Streams and List-based queues can deliver duplicates — network retries, consumer crashes mid-ACK, or XAUTOCLAIM reassignments all cause reprocessing. The fix: gate every consumer on a Redis SET-based idempotency key before doing work.

Use SETNX (or SET ... NX EX) with a TTL tied to your message retention window. When a consumer picks up a message, it atomically attempts to claim the message ID as a key. If the key already exists, the message was already processed — skip it and ACK. If not, process and then ACK with XACK.

For Streams specifically, use the message ID (e.g. 1700000000000-0) as the idempotency key — it's globally unique per stream. For LPOP/RPOP queues where you control message shape, embed a UUID in the payload and use that.

Keep TTLs at least 2× your consumer SLA. Use a Lua script to atomically check-and-set + process if you need the entire operation to be atomic. Avoid using INCR counters as idempotency guards — they don't survive TTL expiry correctly under retry storms.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Lua script: atomic idempotency check + claim
local key = 'idempotent:' .. ARGV[1]  -- ARGV[1] = message ID
local ttl = tonumber(ARGV[2])          -- e.g. 86400 seconds

local result = redis.call('SET', key, '1', 'NX', 'EX', ttl)
if result then
  return 1  -- claimed: safe to process
else
  return 0  -- duplicate: skip and ACK
end

bytes.topic: Dead Letter Queues — poison message handling, retry strategies with backoff
bytes.title: Redis Dead Letter Queues — Poison Message Handling with LMOVE and Backoff
bytes.body:
Redis has no native DLQ, but you can build one with LMOVE and sorted sets. The pattern: workers LMOVE jobs from a pending list to a processing list atomically. On failure, increment a retry counter in a hash and re-queue with exponential backoff using ZADD on a delay queue — scored by Unix timestamp for when the job should next run. A separate scheduler process calls ZRANGEBYSCORE delay_queue 0 <now> periodically and LMOVE ready jobs back to pending.

Once retries exceed a threshold (e.g., 3), LMOVE the message to a dead:queue list instead of re-scheduling. This isolates poison messages without blocking healthy consumers. Store failure context alongside the job in a hash keyed by job ID so you can inspect and replay from the DLQ manually via LMOVE dead:queue pending RIGHT LEFT.

Use MULTI/EXEC to keep counter increments and re-queues atomic. Avoid RPOPLPUSH — it's deprecated; LMOVE with LEFT/RIGHT direction args is the modern equivalent since Redis 6.2.
bytes.code_snippet:
-- Lua script: attempt job, retry with backoff, or DLQ
local job_id = ARGV[1]
local max_retries = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local now = tonumber(ARGV[3])

local retries = tonumber(redis.call('HINCRBY', 'job:meta:' .. job_id, 'retries', 1))

if retries >= max_retries then
  redis.call('LMOVE', 'queue:processing', 'queue:dead', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT')
  return 'dlq'
end

local backoff = math.pow(2, retries) * 1000  -- ms
local run_at = now + backoff
redis.call('LMOVE', 'queue:processing', 'queue:temp', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT')
redis.call('ZADD', 'queue:delay', run_at, job_id)
return 'retrying'

bytes.topic: Redis as Queue — pub/sub vs streams vs lists, persistence trade-offs
bytes.title: Redis as a Queue — Lists vs Pub/Sub vs Streams
bytes.body: Redis offers three distinct primitives for message passing, each with sharp trade-offs. LPUSH/BRPOP on a List is the simplest queue: consumers block-wait with BRPOP, messages are consumed exactly once, but if no consumer is alive, messages sit in the list until one reconnects. There's no fan-out — one message, one consumer. PUBLISH/SUBSCRIBE inverts this: fan-out is native, but messages are fire-and-forget. If a subscriber is offline when PUBLISH fires, the message is lost — no buffering, no persistence. Redis Streams (XADD/XREADGROUP) are the production-grade choice. Each message gets a unique auto-generated ID (e.g. 1712345678901-0), consumer groups track per-consumer ACK state via XACK, and unacknowledged messages surface in the PEL (Pending Entries List) via XPENDING. With AOF persistence and MAXLEN trimming, Streams behave like a durable, replayable log. For job queues, prefer Streams. For ephemeral notifications, use Pub/Sub. Lists work for simple FIFO with a single consumer pool.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Producer
XADD jobs * type resize url https://img.example.com/photo.jpg

# Create consumer group (read from beginning)
XGROUP CREATE jobs workers $ MKSTREAM

# Consumer reads 1 pending message
XREADGROUP GROUP workers consumer-1 COUNT 1 BLOCK 5000 STREAMS jobs >

# Acknowledge after processing
XACK jobs workers 1712345678901-0

# Inspect unacknowledged (stuck) messages
XPENDING jobs workers - + 10

bytes.topic: Redis as Cache — TTL, eviction policies, cache-aside vs write-through vs write-behind
bytes.title: Redis as Cache — TTL, Eviction Policies, and the Three Write Strategies
bytes.body:
Redis gives you precise control over cache lifetime and memory pressure. Set TTL at write time with `SET key value EX 300` or update it later with `EXPIRE key 300`. Use `EXPIREAT` for absolute Unix timestamps when coordinating across services.

When memory hits `maxmemory`, Redis evicts based on your configured policy. `allkeys-lru` is the go-to for general caches — it evicts the least-recently-used key across all keyspace. Use `volatile-lru` if only TTL-bearing keys should be eligible. `allkeys-lfu` (Least Frequently Used, available since 4.0) beats LRU for hot-key-heavy workloads. Never run Redis as a cache without setting `maxmemory` and a policy — unbounded growth will OOM your instance.

For write strategy: **cache-aside** (lazy loading) reads miss to DB then populates Redis — your app owns the logic, stale data is possible. **Write-through** writes to Redis and DB synchronously on every mutation — consistent but adds write latency. **Write-behind** (write-back) buffers writes in Redis and flushes async — highest throughput, but you risk data loss on crash. Redis Streams or a Lua script can implement write-behind durably.
bytes.code_snippet:
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Cache-aside: read-through on miss
def get_user(user_id: str) -> dict:
    key = f"user:{user_id}"
    cached = r.get(key)
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)
    user = db.fetch_user(user_id)          # miss → hit DB
    r.set(key, json.dumps(user), ex=300)   # populate with 5-min TTL
    return user

# Eviction policy (set in redis.conf or at runtime)
# CONFIG SET maxmemory 256mb
# CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru

bytes.topic: BullMQ — job priorities, rate limiting, repeatable jobs, job dependencies
bytes.title: Redis + BullMQ — Job Priorities, Rate Limiting, Repeatable Jobs, and Dependencies
bytes.body:
BullMQ stores every job as a Redis hash and uses sorted sets (ZSET) to implement its queue mechanics — making Redis data structures first-class citizens, not an implementation detail.

Job priorities use a ZSET keyed `bull:{queue}:wait`, where the score is the priority value. Lower score = processed first. Assign `priority: 1` for critical jobs; default is 0 (FIFO within the same priority).

Rate limiting is enforced via a Redis token-bucket pattern. Set `{ limiter: { max: 100, duration: 60000 } }` on the worker — BullMQ writes a rolling window key in Redis and blocks processing when the bucket is exhausted, without dropping jobs.

Repeatable jobs persist their cron definition in `bull:{queue}:repeat` (another ZSET scored by next-run timestamp). BullMQ's scheduler polls this set and re-enqueues automatically — survives worker restarts because state lives in Redis.

Job dependencies use `FlowProducer`, which atomically inserts a parent job into `bull:{queue}:waiting-children` via a Lua script. The parent only moves to the active set once all child job IDs are removed from that key — coordinated entirely through Redis key operations.

bytes.topic: Kafka — partitions, consumer groups, offset management, log compaction
bytes.title: Redis Streams — Kafka Concepts Mapped to XREADGROUP, PEL, and XTRIM
bytes.body:
Redis Streams mirror Kafka's architecture but run in-process. Unlike Pub/Sub (fire-and-forget), Streams are persistent, replayable, and support consumer groups via XGROUP CREATE.

Partitions in Redis are manual — shard across multiple stream keys (events:0, events:1) and route by key hash. No automatic partition assignment exists.

Consumer groups let multiple consumers compete for the same stream. Each consumer calls XREADGROUP with > to claim new, undelivered messages. Unlike Kafka, there's no partition-to-consumer binding — any consumer in the group can receive any message.

Offset management uses entry IDs (e.g. 1526919030474-55). XREADGROUP tracks a Pending Entries List (PEL) per consumer. XACK removes an entry from the PEL — equivalent to committing a Kafka offset. Unacked entries stay in PEL and can be reclaimed via XAUTOCLAIM after a visibility timeout, preventing silent message loss on consumer crash.

Log compaction has no direct equivalent. XTRIM MAXLEN ~ 10000 (approximate, O(1) amortized) caps stream length by age, not by key deduplication — closer to Kafka retention than compaction.
bytes.code_snippet:
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

# Create consumer group — id='0' replays all history, id='$' starts fresh
r.xgroup_create('events', 'workers', id='$', mkstream=True)

# Claim up to 10 new messages; block 2s if stream is empty
messages = r.xreadgroup('workers', 'consumer-1', {'events': '>'}, count=10, block=2000)

for stream, entries in (messages or []):
    for entry_id, data in entries:
        process(data)
        r.xack('events', 'workers', entry_id)  # commit offset

# Reclaim messages stuck in PEL > 30s (dead consumer recovery)
claimed = r.xautoclaim('events', 'workers', 'consumer-1', min_idle_time=30000, start_id='0-0')

# Cap stream length — approximate flag avoids full scan
r.xtrim('events', maxlen=10000, approximate=True)

bytes.topic: Celery — task routing, beat scheduler, result backends, task chaining
bytes.title: Redis-Backed Celery — Task Routing, Beat Scheduling, and Result Chains
bytes.body: When Redis is both your Celery broker and result backend, tasks land in Redis lists (one per queue) and results are stored as Redis keys with a configurable TTL. Route tasks to named queues by setting `task_routes` — Celery pushes to `celery:default`, `celery:priority`, etc. as plain Redis list keys via LPUSH/BRPOP. The beat scheduler persists its state in `celerybeat-schedule` (a shelve file) by default, but with `django-celery-beat` it stores schedules in your DB — Redis just carries the dispatched tasks. For result backends, set `result_expires` to control how long Redis holds task state keys (default 24 h); use `AsyncResult(task_id).get()` to block on completion or `.state` to poll. Chain tasks with `.si()` (immutable signature) to prevent upstream results bleeding into downstream args — critical when Redis result keys hold large payloads you don't want auto-forwarded. Use `chord` for fan-out/fan-in: N parallel tasks finish, Redis accumulates results, callback fires once all keys are resolved.
bytes.code_snippet:
from celery import Celery, chain, chord

app = Celery('worker',
    broker='redis://localhost:6379/0',
    backend='redis://localhost:6379/1')

app.conf.update(
    task_routes={
        'tasks.send_email': {'queue': 'priority'},
        'tasks.generate_report': {'queue': 'default'},
    },
    result_expires=3600,  # Redis TTL for result keys
)

@app.task
def fetch(user_id): return user_id

@app.task
def process(user_id): return f'done:{user_id}'

# Chain: fetch result is NOT passed to process (immutable)
pipeline = chain(fetch.si(42), process.si(42))
result = pipeline.apply_async()
print(result.get())  # blocks until Redis result key resolves

bytes.topic: RabbitMQ — exchanges, routing keys, acknowledgements, prefetch count
bytes.title: Redis Streams — Exchanges, Routing Keys, and Acknowledgements Without RabbitMQ
bytes.body:
Redis Streams give you RabbitMQ-style message queuing natively. Instead of exchanges and bindings, you route by stream key — `XADD orders:created * event order_placed id 123` writes to the `orders:created` stream (your exchange + routing key collapsed into one). Consumer groups (`XGROUP CREATE`) act as competing-consumer queues: each group receives every message, but within a group only one consumer processes it.

Prefetch count maps directly to the `COUNT` argument in `XREADGROUP`. Passing `COUNT 10` fetches at most 10 unacknowledged messages per call — same backpressure semantics as RabbitMQ's `basic.qos`. The `>` cursor means "only new, undelivered messages"; passing a specific ID re-reads entries already in the Pending Entry List (PEL).

Acknowledgement is explicit: `XACK orders:created workers <msg-id>` removes the entry from the PEL. Unacked messages linger there and can be stolen by another consumer via `XCLAIM` after an idle timeout — your dead-letter/requeue path. Redis has no native nack; on failure, manually `XADD` to a dead-letter stream before `XACK`ing the original.
bytes.code_snippet:
import redis

r = redis.Redis(decode_responses=True)

# Publisher: stream key acts as exchange + routing key
r.xadd("orders:created", {"event": "order_placed", "id": "123"})

# One-time consumer group setup
r.xgroup_create("orders:created", "workers", id="0", mkstream=True)

# Fetch up to 10 msgs (COUNT = prefetch limit)
messages = r.xreadgroup("workers", "consumer1", {"orders:created": ">"}, count=10, block=5000)

for _stream, entries in (messages or []):
    for msg_id, data in entries:
        try:
            process(data)
            r.xack("orders:created", "workers", msg_id)  # explicit ack
        except Exception:
            r.xadd("orders:dead", {**data, "failed_id": msg_id})  # manual DLQ
            r.xack("orders:created", "workers", msg_id)

bytes.topic: Ordering — when order matters, partitioning strategy for ordered processing
bytes.title: Redis Streams — Partitioning Strategy for Ordered Message Processing
bytes.body: Redis Streams guarantee per-stream ordering via auto-generated entry IDs (millsecond-sequence format: `1700000000000-0`). But a single stream becomes a bottleneck under high throughput — one consumer group serializes processing. The solution: partition by a stable key (user ID, tenant ID, order ID) using modulo hashing across N streams. `XADD orders:stream:{userId % 8} * payload data` ensures all events for a given entity land in the same stream, preserving causal order without cross-stream coordination. Consumers use `XREADGROUP GROUP workers consumer1 COUNT 10 STREAMS orders:stream:0 >` per partition. Increase N by doubling and remapping — existing streams drain naturally before new ones take over. Avoid partitioning by timestamp; clock skew destroys ordering guarantees. Use `XPENDING` to detect stalled messages per partition and `XCLAIM` to reassign them after a visibility timeout. The entry ID's monotonic sequence component handles sub-millisecond bursts on the same stream without collision, so you never need application-level sequence numbers.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Partition writes by entity key
PARTITIONS = 8

def publish_event(redis_client, user_id: int, payload: dict):
    stream_key = f"orders:stream:{user_id % PARTITIONS}"
    redis_client.xadd(stream_key, payload)

# Consumer reads from a specific partition
def consume_partition(redis_client, partition: int, group: str, consumer: str):
    stream_key = f"orders:stream:{partition}"
    messages = redis_client.xreadgroup(
        groupname=group,
        consumername=consumer,
        streams={stream_key: ">"},
        count=10,
        block=2000
    )
    return messages

bytes.topic: Scaling — horizontal consumer scaling, partition count, rebalancing
bytes.title: Redis Streams — Horizontal Consumer Scaling with Consumer Groups
bytes.body:
Redis Streams' consumer group model is the right primitive for horizontally scaling message processing. Unlike Pub/Sub (which fans out every message to every subscriber), a consumer group distributes messages across consumers — each message is delivered to exactly one consumer in the group.

Use `XREADGROUP` with a unique consumer name per instance. Redis tracks pending entries per consumer in the PEL (Pending Entries List). If a consumer dies mid-processing, another can claim its stalled messages via `XAUTOCLAIM` after a visibility timeout.

There are no partitions in Redis Streams the way Kafka has them — a single stream is the unit of distribution. To scale beyond what one stream can handle, manually shard: route by `user_id % N` into streams like `events:0`, `events:1`, ..., `events:N-1`, and assign dedicated consumer groups per shard. Adding shards requires rebalancing your routing logic and spinning up new consumer group instances — Redis itself does not rebalance automatically. Keep shard count stable; resharding means rehashing keys and migrating in-flight PEL entries manually.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Create group on stream ($ = only new messages)
XGROUP CREATE events:0 workers $ MKSTREAM

# Consumer reads up to 10 messages, blocks 2s if empty
XREADGROUP GROUP workers consumer-1 COUNT 10 BLOCK 2000 STREAMS events:0 >

# Acknowledge processed message
XACK events:0 workers 1712000000000-0

# Claim messages pending > 30s from dead consumers
XAUTOCLAIM events:0 workers consumer-2 30000 0-0 COUNT 50

bytes.topic: NATS — JetStream, subject-based routing, at-least-once delivery
bytes.title: Redis Streams Developer's Intro to NATS JetStream — Subjects, Durables, and At-Least-Once
bytes.body:
If you rely on Redis Streams (XADD/XREAD/XACK) for durable messaging, NATS JetStream maps cleanly to those semantics — but with subject-based routing instead of per-key streams.

In Redis, a consumer group reads from a named stream key and calls XACK to confirm delivery. JetStream works the same way: create a Stream bound to one or more subjects (e.g., orders.>), attach a durable Consumer, and messages stay in the pending-entry list until your handler calls msg.Ack() — identical intent to Redis's PEL.

Where Redis requires separate stream keys or Lua fan-out for routing, NATS subjects use wildcards natively: orders.* matches one token, orders.> matches all descendants. One JetStream Stream can capture orders.created, orders.shipped, and orders.cancelled with no extra keys.

JetStream retention policies also map to familiar Redis patterns: work-queue retention deletes a message after the first ack (single-consumer XDEL-after-XACK), while interest-based retention waits until every bound consumer acks (multi-consumer fan-out). For Redis developers hitting keyspace routing limits, JetStream's durable consumers and hierarchical subjects cover the gap without touching your Redis cluster.
bytes.code_snippet:
import nats
import asyncio

async def main():
    nc = await nats.connect("nats://localhost:4222")
    js = nc.jetstream()

    # Equivalent to: XADD orders * event placed
    await js.add_stream(name="orders", subjects=["orders.>"])
    await js.publish("orders.created", b'{"id": 42}')

    # Equivalent to: XREADGROUP + XACK
    sub = await js.subscribe("orders.>", durable="order-processor")
    msg = await sub.next_msg(timeout=2.0)
    print(msg.data)          # b'{"id": 42}'
    await msg.ack()          # Clears PEL entry — like XACK mystream grp <id>

asyncio.run(main())

bytes.topic: Monitoring — queue depth alerts, consumer lag, dead letter queue alerts
bytes.title: Redis Queue Monitoring — Depth Alerts, Consumer Lag, and Dead Letter Queues
bytes.body: Redis streams (`XLEN`, `XPENDING`) and list-based queues (`LLEN`) expose the metrics you need for production alerting. For list queues, `LLEN myqueue` gives depth instantly — alert when it exceeds your SLA threshold. For Redis Streams, `XPENDING mystream mygroup - + 10` reveals unacknowledged messages per consumer group; consumer lag is `last_delivered_id` vs the stream's last entry ID, computable via `XINFO GROUPS mystream`. Dead letter queues (DLQs) are convention-based in Redis: after N failed `XACK` attempts, move the message to a `mystream:dlq` stream using `XADD`. Monitor DLQ depth with `XLEN mystream:dlq` and alert on any non-zero value. For Prometheus scraping, `redis_exporter` exposes `redis_stream_length`, `redis_stream_groups_pending`, and `redis_connected_clients`. Pair with Grafana alerts: fire if `xpending > 500` for 5 minutes or `dlq_length > 0`. Polling via `XINFO STREAM` in a sidecar loop works for lightweight setups without a full exporter.
bytes.code_snippet:
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

STREAM = 'jobs'
GROUP = 'workers'
DLQ = 'jobs:dlq'
DEPTH_THRESHOLD = 500

# Queue depth
depth = r.xlen(STREAM)
if depth > DEPTH_THRESHOLD:
    print(f'ALERT: queue depth {depth} exceeds threshold')

# Consumer lag per group
groups = r.xinfo_groups(STREAM)
for g in groups:
    lag = g['lag']  # Redis 7.0+
    pending = g['pending']
    print(f"{g['name']}: lag={lag}, pending={pending}")

# DLQ alert
dlq_len = r.xlen(DLQ)
if dlq_len > 0:
    print(f'ALERT: {dlq_len} messages in DLQ — manual intervention required')

bytes.topic: Backpressure — what happens when consumers lag, preventing memory blowout
bytes.title: Redis Streams Backpressure — Capping Memory When Consumers Lag
bytes.body:
When consumers fall behind in Redis Streams, the stream grows unbounded and your instance OOMs. Redis gives you two levers: producer-side trimming and consumer-side lag visibility.

Use `XADD mystream MAXLEN ~ 10000 * field value` to cap the stream. The `~` lets Redis trim approximately, deferring exact radix-tree rebalancing — far cheaper than strict `MAXLEN 10000` on every write.

Lag lives in the Pending Entry List (PEL). Every message delivered via `XREADGROUP` sits there until `XACK`'d. Run `XPENDING mystream mygroup - + 10` to inspect stale entries. A swelling PEL means consumers are crashing before acking, or processing too slowly.

For dead consumers, `XAUTOCLAIM mystream mygroup recovery 60000 0-0` atomically moves messages idle over 60 seconds from the dead consumer's PEL to a recovery consumer — no manual `XCLAIM` loop needed. This prevents indefinite accumulation from a crashed worker.

For Pub/Sub, set `client-output-buffer-limit pubsub 32mb 8mb 60` in `redis.conf` — slow subscribers are disconnected before they exhaust memory, protecting the rest of the instance.
bytes.code_snippet:
import redis

r = redis.Redis(decode_responses=True)

# Producer: bound stream to ~10k entries
r.xadd("jobs", {"task": "resize", "id": "99"}, maxlen=10000, approximate=True)

# Consumer: read and ack
for stream, entries in r.xreadgroup("workers", "w1", {"jobs": ">"}, count=10, block=5000) or []:
    for msg_id, data in entries:
        process(data)  # your handler
        r.xack("jobs", "workers", msg_id)

# Inspect lag
print(r.xpending("jobs", "workers"))  # {'pending': N, 'min': '...', 'max': '...'}

# Reclaim messages idle > 60s from dead consumers
next_id, claimed, _ = r.xautoclaim("jobs", "workers", "recovery", min_idle_time=60000, start_id="0-0")

bytes.topic: When to Use What — Redis vs Kafka vs RabbitMQ decision framework
bytes.title: Redis vs Kafka vs RabbitMQ — A Decision Framework Built Around Redis
bytes.body:
Redis Streams, Kafka, and RabbitMQ solve overlapping problems but with fundamentally different trade-offs. Redis is the right default for most teams — until it isn't.

Use Redis Streams (`XADD`/`XREADGROUP`) when you need lightweight pub/sub with consumer groups, message acknowledgment (`XACK`), and replay within a short retention window. It's ideal for real-time notifications, job queues under moderate volume, and systems already running Redis. Persistence is configurable via AOF/RDB, but it's not the primary guarantee.

Switch to Kafka when you need indefinite log retention, high-throughput event sourcing (millions/sec), or cross-team data pipelines. Kafka's consumer offset model enables replay at scale — Redis can't match this economically at high cardinality.

Choose RabbitMQ when routing logic is complex — topic exchanges, dead-letter queues with fine-grained TTL per message, or AMQP compliance is required. RabbitMQ's `x-dead-letter-exchange` header gives you built-in retry routing Redis Streams lacks natively.

The heuristic: start with Redis Streams. Migrate to Kafka at sustained >100k msg/sec or when retention > 7 days becomes a hard requirement.

bytes.topic: Security — broker authentication, TLS, network isolation
bytes.title: Redis Security — TLS, ACLs, and Network Isolation Done Right
bytes.body:
Running Redis without authentication is a P0 incident waiting to happen. Start with ACL users: replace the legacy `requirepass` with fine-grained ACL rules so each service gets only the commands it needs. A background worker that only enqueues jobs should have `+LPUSH +RPUSH ~queue:*` and nothing else — no `FLUSHDB`, no `CONFIG`, no `KEYS`.

For transport, enable TLS by setting `tls-port 6380`, pointing `tls-cert-file`, `tls-key-file`, and `tls-ca-cert-file` in `redis.conf`. Clients must present a cert (`tls-auth-clients yes`) — mutual TLS prevents rogue clients from connecting even inside a VPC.

Network isolation is your last line of defense: bind Redis to a private IP only (`bind 10.0.0.5 127.0.0.1`), never `0.0.0.0`. Use a security group or `iptables` to whitelist only app-tier IPs on port 6379/6380. Enable `protected-mode yes` so Redis refuses connections on non-loopback interfaces without a password. Combine ACLs + mTLS + network allowlists — each layer stops a different attack vector.
bytes.code_snippet:
# redis.conf — production hardening
bind 10.0.0.5 127.0.0.1
protected-mode yes

tls-port 6380
port 0  # disable plaintext
tls-cert-file /etc/redis/tls/redis.crt
tls-key-file  /etc/redis/tls/redis.key
tls-ca-cert-file /etc/redis/tls/ca.crt
tls-auth-clients yes

# ACL: worker can only push to queue:* keys
ACL SETUSER worker on >s3cr3t ~queue:* +LPUSH +RPUSH +LLEN -@all
ACL SETUSER readonly on >r0pwd ~* +GET +MGET +HGET -@all
ACL SETUSER default off  # disable anonymous access

bytes.topic: Testing — testing consumers in isolation, mocking brokers in tests
bytes.title: Redis Streams — Testing Consumers in Isolation with fakeredis
bytes.body:
Testing a Redis Streams consumer end-to-end requires a live broker, but unit-testing the processing logic doesn't. The pattern: inject the Redis connection, swap it for `fakeredis.FakeRedis()` in tests, then seed messages directly with `XADD` before invoking your consumer loop.

`fakeredis` fully implements `XADD`, `XREADGROUP`, `XACK`, and `XPENDING`, so consumer group mechanics — including the pending-entries list (PEL) — behave identically to a real Redis instance. This means you can assert not just that your handler ran, but that `XACK` was called and the PEL is empty afterward.

For failure paths, seed a message, make your handler throw, then call `XPENDING` to verify the message stays in the PEL and gets re-delivered on the next `XREADGROUP` poll. This catches the silent bug where a bad handler accidentally acks before processing finishes.

Avoid mocking at the `IDatabase` or `redis.client` level — you'll spend more effort maintaining the mock than the test is worth. `fakeredis` gives you a real state machine without a Docker dependency.
